title: Munich court to rule on GEMA v. Suno on July 31 in first European AI music training test
subtitle: The German collecting society is suing Suno over six songs including 'Forever Young' and 'Mambo No. 5' — a plaintiff win would be the first EU ruling that training on copyrighted music requires a license.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: The Munich Regional Court's 42nd Civil Chamber is scheduled to deliver its verdict in GEMA v. Suno on July 31. GEMA — Germany's mechanical rights society, representing composers, lyricists, and music publishers — filed the case in January 2025 alleging that Suno trained its music generation model on copyrighted works without a license. The disputed set is six songs: 'Forever Young' and 'Big in Japan' by Alphaville, 'Atemlos' by Kristina Bach, 'Mambo No. 5' by Lou Bega, and 'Rasputin' and 'Daddy Cool' by Boney M. The court held oral hearings on March 9, moved the verdict from June 12 to July 31 for administrative reasons, and has confirmed the new date will hold.

The ruling matters because European AI copyright law is currently held together by an untested exception. The EU AI Act and the 2019 Digital Single Market Directive together let commercial rightsholders opt out of text-and-data mining. GEMA argues that Suno never checked the opt-outs, and separately that Suno's outputs sometimes reproduce recognizable elements of the training data, which would push the case out of the mining exception entirely. A GEMA win would be the first European decision that AI music training requires licensing authorization — and under German law it could be enforced while Suno appeals, which would immediately reshape how any generative music company operates in the EU.

The context in the US is very different. In Sony Music v. Suno in Massachusetts, the schedule now runs fact discovery to September 30 and dispositive motions to April 9, 2027, so a US decision is at least a year away. Warner settled with Suno in November 2025. Universal settled with Udio in October 2025 and is co-building a joint AI music platform. Sony is now the last major label still litigating in the US against Suno, and GEMA is effectively the last major European rights body in court on this question. The Munich ruling is the first one either side will actually get to point to.

For learners: the interesting fight in AI copyright is not whether a chatbot memorized a book. It is whether a company that trained on data it did not license can keep operating while the case is pending. GEMA's request in Munich includes an injunction, not just damages. Watch how the court handles that specific relief. If Suno keeps operating under a favorable outcome, other generative-audio companies will read the ruling as permission. If Suno is forced to pause EU service pending appeal, the next round of AI music startups will build with licensing baked in from day one. That distinction — allowed to keep running or not — is where the practical rules of the road get written.

sources.title: GEMA, Suno copyright ruling postponed by Munich court to July 31 — MLex
